Faction.name: {=!}Ghilman
Faction.text: {=!}The Ghilman trace their origins to the ancient tradition of the ghulam, the slave-soldier caste that once formed the backbone of every great sultanate in Hind. Each warrior pledges his sword to the order and in doing so becomes its servant and co-master. They are known across the subcontinent for their oiled turbans, peacock feathers, and fine silks. But no lord should mistake their elegance for weakness — as mounted archers they are peerless, and their ability to fight as heavy lancers while maintaining the mobility of light cavalry has confounded armies far larger than their own band.

Faction.name: {=!}Pindari Risala
Faction.text: {=!}The Pindaris began as camp followers and spare cavalry attached to the great Maratha armies. When the wars died down they found themselves masterless — too many swords, too little coin. What began as small groups of discharged horsemen grew into a vast brotherhood of plunderers drawn from every caste and creed: Afghan riders, Rajput adventurers, displaced Mughal sepoys. They raid with terrifying speed, stripping a village bare before the garrison can mount a defense. Every great kingdom curses and employs them in equal measure; a Pindari chieftain's loyalty lasts precisely as long as the gold holds out.

Faction.name: {=!}Rohilla Sardars
Faction.text: {=!}The Rohillas are the sons and grandsons of Afghan mercenaries who descended through the Khyber Pass seeking fortune and found a homeland in the fertile Gangetic plains. They have settled in Rohilkhand and built their own petty fiefdoms, but their warrior traditions remain unchanged from the high mountains. They are disciplined fighters who combine Afghan sword-and-shield tactics with the long-barreled jezail musket, and their unit cohesion in the face of cavalry charges is legendary. Their services command a premium price, for a Rohilla warband holds its ground with a stubbornness that neither numbers nor artillery can entirely shake.

Faction.name: {=!}Dakkani Bargirs
Faction.text: {=!}The Dakkani Bargirs are hardened veterans of a lifetime of Deccan warfare, men who have served the Adil Shahs, the Mughals, and the Marathas alike and now sell their swords to whoever offers coin. They are not glory-seekers but consummate professionals who hold their contracts sacred and regard any employer foolish enough to underpay them with absolute contempt. Their specialty is siege and garrison work, and after decades of campaigns across the plateau they carry in their heads a comprehensive map of every fortification, water source, and mountain pass between the Narmada and the Kaveri.

Faction.name: {=!}Thuggee Brotherhood
Faction.text: {=!}The Thuggee Brotherhood are the terror of every caravan road in the subcontinent. They travel disguised as pilgrims, merchants, or soldiers, infiltrating convoys over days or weeks while they identify their marks. Then, at a signal known only to initiates, they strike simultaneously — strangling victims with the rumal, the ritual yellow scarf, in service to Kali. They believe sincerely that they perform sacred duty with every kill. No merchant who values his life travels the long roads without an armed escort, and the mere rumor of Thuggees on a road is enough to halt commerce for weeks.

Faction.name: {=!}Gond Pahari
Faction.text: {=!}The Gond are the ancient masters of the great forests of central Hind, a warrior tribe that has never submitted to any empire. They live by the old laws of the jungle — raiding, hunting, and protecting their sacred groves from encroaching plains settlers. Their knowledge of the forest is absolute; they can move through dense jungle without trace and materialize from nowhere to overwhelm soldiers who believed the road was safe. Plains lords who have tried to bring them under control learned through bitter experience that war against the Gond in their own forests is a costly fool's errand.

Faction.name: {=!}Zamindar Outlaws
Faction.text: {=!}The Zamindar Outlaws began as a righteous cause — local landholders who refused to submit to the crushing demands of imperial revenue collectors, hiding in the hills and sheltering their tenants from the mansabdars. Over the years the movement grew indistinguishable from plain banditry. They now extort the same peasants they once protected and maintain corrupt arrangements with local officials who take a share of the proceeds. Their leaders still speak the old language of resistance and justice, but the reality of their operations is considerably less noble than their founding myth.

Faction.name: {=!}Andhera Qabza
Faction.text: {=!}The Andhera Qabza, the Dark Grip, are the unseen muscle beneath every major trading city and caravan route in the realm. They are not romantic outlaws but a ruthlessly efficient criminal network that has made itself indispensable to the powerful — suppressing riots on behalf of nervous governors, disappearing troublesome preachers for anxious nobles, and managing illicit trade with an efficiency the customs houses envy. A merchant who pays the Qabza's toll travels safely; one who refuses is unlikely to complete his journey.

Faction.name: {=!}Daryai Dakait
Faction.text: {=!}The Daryai Dakait rule the network of rivers, marshes, and delta channels that honest merchants must use to move goods across the subcontinent. They operate from hidden islands and muddy inlets along the Ganga and its tributaries, using shallow boats to intercept cargo vessels before they reach their destinations. Their ranks are filled with runaway serfs, displaced fishermen, and veterans of river battles who chose piracy over starvation. River lords long ago learned that a campaign to clear the Dakait usually accomplishes nothing beyond driving them deeper into the delta, where no army can follow.

Faction.name: {=!}Mahdawi Darwesh
Faction.text: {=!}The Mahdawi Darwesh are the scattered remnants of a messianic movement that once shook the foundations of the old Sultanate, when a wandering saint proclaimed himself the Mahdi and gathered thousands of followers who renounced all worldly goods to follow him. The armies of the powerful broke the movement, but could not extinguish its fire. Now scattered in small secretive communities across the subcontinent, the Darwesh keep the faith alive — and sustain themselves by collecting what they call righteous tribute from those who have strayed from the true path, awaiting the day when the Mahdi returns to reclaim the lands of the faithful.

Faction.name: {=!}Banjara Tanda
Faction.text: {=!}The Banjara Tanda — a tanda being their word for a nomadic band — are the great wandering merchant-warriors of the subcontinent. Their enormous caravans of laden oxen carry salt, grain, and forest goods across every kingdom. For centuries the great armies depended on the Banjara for their supply lines, and every empire has needed their services. Yet the Banjara have never submitted to any master — they pay no taxes, acknowledge no lord, and their seasonal paths cut across the borders of every kingdom as though those borders do not exist. They are simultaneously indispensable and ungovernable.

Faction.name: {=!}Kuchi Qabilas
Faction.text: {=!}The Kuchi Qabilas are the free nomads of the northwestern frontier, moving their vast flocks of sheep and camels between the summer pastures of the high mountain valleys and the winter plains of the Indus. They acknowledge no settled authority and cross borders as freely as the wind. Their young men are accomplished horsemen and marksmen, hardened by lives spent protecting flocks from wolves, raiders, and rivals. Every settled kingdom has found that taxing or conscripting the Kuchis is more trouble than it is worth — they simply vanish into the mountains and reappear the following season in a different valley entirely.

Faction.name: {=!}Bhil Pahari
Faction.text: {=!}The Bhil Pahari are the ancient masters of the Aravalli and Vindhya hill forests, a tribal people who have lived outside every known tax system and beyond the reach of any landlord's records since before memory. They tend their plots by cutting and burning, moving on when the soil is exhausted, living lightly on the land and invisibly to the state. Plains lords who attempt to bring them under control find the Bhil melting into the jungle like smoke, only to reappear months later in a different valley. They are masterful bowmen and trackers, and their intimate knowledge of the hills makes them dangerous enemies and invaluable allies.

Faction.name: {=!}Palayakkarar
Faction.text: {=!}The Palayakkarar are the warrior-chieftains of the deep south, holders of ancient grants who have never fully accepted the authority of any empire. Each Palayam is a small fortress-domain, and its lord is simultaneously tax-collector, judge, and warlord. For generations they supplied soldiers and tribute to whichever great power demanded it, while maintaining their own armies and settling their own quarrels with their own blades. As the great empires have weakened, the Palayakkarar have grown bolder — collecting their own revenues and administering their territories in ways that leave imperial officials little role to play.
